content:
Hi! I've been writing up a rust interface for KMS drivers written in rust, and little while ago I had asked for some advice regarding https://rust-for-linux.zulipchat.com/#narrow/stream/415254-DRM/topic/Early.20drm_plane_state.20bindings.20feedback
I was skimming through the wiki again this morning and I think I answered my question, mainly it looks like what I probably want for this is some sort of LockedBy equivalent that can handle ww_mutex so that I can make structs like Plane<T> opaque until the caller can actually prove they hold the right locks to access it's contents.
I'm now looking at implementing wrappers for ww_mutex, but since this lock acquisition scheme is not infallible (see https://lwn.net/Articles/548909/ ) it doesn't seem like sync::lock::Backend would be suitable for this. Which brings me to ask: if I'm writing such wrappers, should I start by introducing a new variant of sync::lock::Backend designed for locks with fallible acquisition schemes? Or should I just go straight to writing a wrapper for ww_mutex, and worry about moving this stuff out to a trait later if we end up finding more users?

I don't think that the current lock design can cover ww_mutex. One important in ww_mutex is the ww_acquire_ctx, and Rust API will need to design the interface where ctx interact with mutex. Also what ww_mutexAPI implies is the user somehow tracks all the acquired locks (so that they can be unlocked when "wound"), for a safe API, I think you will need to maintain that in an vector-like data structure in the ctx.

Boqun Feng said:

I don't think that the current lock design can cover ww_mutex. One important in ww_mutex is the ww_acquire_ctx, and Rust API will need to design the interface where ctx interact with mutex. Also what ww_mutexAPI implies is the user somehow tracks all the acquired locks (so that they can be unlocked when "wound"), for a safe API, I think you will need to maintain that in an vector-like data structure in the ctx.

awesome, I figured as much :) - thanks for confirming!
Go ahead, I can help review ;-)

JFYI too: Seems like it might just be for drm_modeset lock for the moment as well instead of ww_mutex generally, since we can't really just use the plain ww mutex API for that (DRM adds some additional debugging functionality to it's modeset locks).
can we expose that lock without any ww_ pieces? I think drm exec also uses ww_ but maybe we also can hide it from drivers there.

Dave Airlie said:

can we expose that lock without any ww_ pieces? I think drm exec also uses ww_ but maybe we also can hide it from drivers there.

Just got out of a meeting but yeah - I think this is actually going to end up being the way to go, because I didnt realize until looking that you don't really directly deal with ww_ at all on the driver's side

in fact we might not even end up with any sort of independent ModesetLock object, and just may end up instead with ModesetGuard or something like that - and then have various modesetting objects with locks implement a common trait that provides methods for attempting to grab their respective locks
(also I should probably note, I don't think I've looked at drm exec at all, although from a quick glance it seems like it's more or less the same deal there - DRM handles creating locks and such)

Hi again! It's been quite a while since the last post I made here. For a while the usecase for having a ww mutex binding ended up not panning out with the KMS bindings, but now that I've been cleaning up the gem shmem patches I've run into some spots where we could eventually use some sort of WwMutex wrapper. At some point I can get to it, but would this possibly be something we could have as a todo item for new contributors also?
Note: it's not super high priority, the unsafe code we have works fine but, well, it is unsafe ;P.
I would like to help with that
(if it's still a TODO thing)

Cc: @Alice Ryhl

Hi @Onur Özkan ,

Yes, it is -- @Daniel Almeida already came across ww_mutex in the context of drm_gpuvm and dma_resv, where the latter can be a very thin abstraction layer above ww_mutex.
I think we should probably move away from the sync::lock::Backend framework anyway. If it doesn't fit ww_mutex, then I would not worry about expanding it - just write a new separate abstraction.
sounds cool, I will spend sometime on this

A status update regarding ww_mutex: I looked into the sync::lock::Backend and the kernel/locking tree today. From what I can see, it's not just limiting ww_mutex, but also other locking primitives under the kernel/locking tree. I plan to refactor it into sync::locking::Backend so it can serve as a proper generic foundation for kernel/locking primitives. This would allow us to abstract more than just ww_mutex, which I plan to do one by one after completing the rewrite/refactor/redesign of sync::lock::Backend. Before going down this path, I wanted to share it here to make sure I am not heading in an unusual or unnecessary direction. Any concerns?

Alice Ryhl said:

I think we should probably move away from the sync::lock::Backend framework anyway. If it doesn't fit ww_mutex, then I would not worry about expanding it - just write a new separate abstraction.

Based on this message, there shouldn't be any problem with my current plan but please correct me if I am wrong :)
I can't answer fully without seeing the abstraction, but I generally believe that locks are currently more abstract than what they need to be.

Instead of making Backend more generic, I would suggest focusing on providing ww_mutex first, some concepts in ww_mutex don’t apply to simple lock interfaces (like spinlock), so let’s not make Backend more complicated.

Alice Ryhl said:

I can't answer fully without seeing the abstraction, but I generally believe that locks are currently more abstract than what they need to be.

I agree with that
E.g. we really don’t need returning a -EDEADLOCK from a lock function, because it can only help ww_mutex, so it makes no sense to abstract Backend to do that.

Boqun Feng said:

E.g. we really don’t need returning a -EDEADLOCK from a lock function, because it can only help ww_mutex, so it makes no sense to abstract Backend to do that.

Yeah that would make no sense to do it that way, best would be adding generic type to the trait. But still, there will be some functions which will be usefuly only for certain backends
I guess I will just skip the current Backend as is

Boqun Feng said:

Instead of making Backend more generic, I would suggest focusing on providing ww_mutex first, some concepts in ww_mutex don’t apply to simple lock interfaces (like spinlock), so let’s not make Backend more complicated.

Agreed

FWIW: for a moment I did actually consider for a moment it might make sense to implement Backend for the case of ww mutexes without specific contexts, but taking a look at the ww mutex code in the kernel it seems that even without a context it's still possible to hit -EDEADLK (though I'm not sure I really understand why)

Okay, so this is the initial patch I'm planning to send for review. Last week, I sent this minor patch, but I'm not sure if I did it properly or if it just takes some time to get a response from reviewers (I haven't seen any response yet).

What I did was simply pick two maintainers from the MAINTAINERS file and run this command:

git send-email --to=mturquette@baylibre.com,sboyd@kernel.org 0001-Various-improvements-on-clock-abstractions.patch

Also, I feel like the ww_mutex abstraction patch is relatively fat (or is it not? I am just too new to patch-based development), so I expect there might be a lot of back-and-forth during review. I'm not sure what the best approach is here, should I wait for some initial feedback on this patch, or just go ahead and send it now?
You need to send the patch to the mailing list so that everyone can see it.

Please see https://rust-for-linux.com/contributing#submitting-patches -- we have an example command there and other pointers (the rest of the page is also important, if you haven't seen it! :)

Thanks!
Oh, seems like I sould re-send the patch with including you to the sending address :)
I missed that part, thank you

Just in case, that is one possible command for the "Rust" subsystem, but you may need to add other entries too like the page mentions, depending on the patch.

For instance, as @Boqun Feng mentioned in the list (https://lore.kernel.org/rust-for-linux/aFBbTMMTHrgHwVFJ@Mac.home/), you want to also add the M/R/L addresses for locking. You can use get_maintainers.pl to search for things and other advanced options.

I hope that helps!
/me offering free Cc list checking ;-)
I would suggest you take Boqun's offer (thanks Boqun) -- Cc list checking is not too fun, and getting those right is important nevertheless :)

Hi, I was going through the v5 review notes and saw that Benno mentioned this:

I don't know the design of struct ww_mutex, but from the code below I gathered that it has some special error return values that signify that one should release other locks.

Did anyone think about making a more Rusty API that would allow one to try to lock multiple mutexes at the same time (in a specified order) and if it fails, it would do the resetting automatically?

As I just recently joined the conversation on Zulip and picked up this task to help out with the Rust side of the kernel, I don't feel like I'm the right person to answer this design question. What should I do at this point?

Also, what should we do when there is a conflict of suggestion coming from 2 different reviewers? (e.g., one suggesting something do add while the other doesn't like it).

It's okay to say no to adding more things to your change.

As for disagreements, one option is to reply and point out the disagreement. The reviewer may not have noticed the disagreement.

Onur Özkan said:

Hi, I was going through the v5 review notes and saw that Benno mentioned this:

I don't know the design of struct ww_mutex, but from the code below I gathered that it has some special error return values that signify that one should release other locks.

Did anyone think about making a more Rusty API that would allow one to try to lock multiple mutexes at the same time (in a specified order) and if it fails, it would do the resetting automatically?

As I just recently joined the conversation on Zulip and picked up this task to help out with the Rust side of the kernel, I don't feel like I'm the right person to answer this design question. What should I do at this point?

Don't worry, I just replied to Benno.

jfyi: We are currently waiting to hear back from the DRM folks about the design of the Rust ww_mutex API before moving forward (see the latest mail in the patch series).

Any input would be appreciated :)

I read through a bit previous discussion, and seems that the ww_mutex requirement comes from the abstraction of drm_plane, @Lyude it'll be nice if you can point us to an example that C code current does locking for drm_plane, which would help on the design of ww_mutex.
I might come up with a couple of design ideas/suggestions in a week or so by checking the C sources that use ww_mutex while we wait for feedback from DRM

Boqun Feng said:

I read through a bit previous discussion, and seems that the ww_mutex requirement comes from the abstraction of drm_plane, Lyude it'll be nice if you can point us to an example that C code current does locking for drm_plane, which would help on the design of ww_mutex.

For drm_plane? That would be on the KMS side of things, and I haven't run into any needs for ww mutexes there quite yet. ww mutexes do get used pretty often in kms but for all of the abstractions I've been writing they're pretty much all called in contexts where all of the locking is handled by the C functions that we call down to
You might be thinking of dma_resv

This being said though, I do have some examples of it being used in KMS:

https://gitlab.freedesktop.org/lyudess/linux/-/blob/rust/irq-spinlock/drivers/gpu/drm/nouveau/dispnv50/crc.c?ref_type=heads#L540

here we setup an atomic display commit, add the relevant resources that we need (each get_*_state() call ends up acquiring a drm_modeset_lock, which is a thin wrapper around ww_mutex that uses the atomic context we initialize in drm_atomic_state). If we deadlock, we clear the atomic state and go back to the start and try again
BTW - it might take me until next week to take a look at this but I'm more then happy to help out with the review of this
